[section label="Contact" bg_overlay="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.488)" padding="90px" padding__sm="60px"] [row h_align="center" padding="0px 0px 0px 0px"] [col span="9" span__sm="12" span__md="8" padding="70px 70px 30px 70px" padding__sm="50px 30px 10px 30px" margin="110px 0px 0px 0px" align="center" bg_color="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.471)" bg_radius="10" text_depth="1" border="0px 0px 0px 0px" border_margin="0px 0px 0px 0px"] [ux_text line_height="1.75" text_color="rgb(0, 0, 0)"]

Origin & Meaning

Traditional Chinese Calligraphy: Dream (夢)
Contemporary Chinese Master Calligrapher
Original Design for Tattoo

This unique calligraphy work features the Traditional Chinese character 夢 (Meng), meaning 'Dream'. It was specially commissioned from a 70-year-old Chinese master calligrapher for use as a tattoo design. The character is written in a powerful, fluid running script (Xingshu), capturing the spirit of aspiration and vision. Beyond its physical beauty, it symbolizes personal hope, ambition, and the profound depth of human imagination, making it a meaningful and culturally rich choice for a permanent body art statement.

[/ux_text] [gap height="10px"] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

Origin & Meaning

Vintage European Style Crown
Rennell Rodd · 1886
British Library

This intricate engraving of a vintage European-style crown was originally published in 1886 as a decorative illustration for Rennell Rodd's collection of poetry, Feda, With Other Poems, Chiefly Lyrical. Sourced from the archives of the British Library, the etching showcases classic royal regalia with finely detailed linework and ornate heraldic elements, characteristic of late 19th-century book art.

[/ux_text] [gap height="10px"] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

post_title: Composition with Red, Yellow, Blue, and Black (1921)
post_content:
[section label="Contact" bg="620" bg_overlay="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.488)" bg_pos="91% 29%" padding="90px" padding__sm="60px"] [row h_align="center" padding="0px 0px 0px 0px"] [col span="9" span__sm="12" span__md="8" padding="70px 70px 30px 70px" padding__sm="50px 30px 10px 30px" margin="110px 0px 0px 0px" align="center" bg_color="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.471)" bg_radius="10" text_depth="1" border="0px 0px 0px 0px" border_margin="0px 0px 0px 0px"] [ux_text line_height="1.75" text_color="rgb(0, 0, 0)"]

Origin & Meaning

Composition with Red, Yellow, Blue, and Black
Piet Mondrian · 1921
Kunstmuseum Den Haag

Painted in 1921, Composition with Red, Yellow, Blue, and Black is a definitive masterpiece of Neoplasticism by Dutch pioneer Piet Mondrian. Moving away from representational art, Mondrian relies strictly on a grid of black lines and asymmetrical planes of primary colors. This work reflects his pursuit of universal harmony and balance, distilling the visual world into its most pure, elemental forms.

[/ux_text] [gap height="10px"] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

Plate 50: Sacred Heart
Unknown American 20th Century · 1935–1942
National Gallery of Art

Part of the portfolio Spanish Colonial Designs of New Mexico, this rendering depicts a traditional "Sacred Heart" motif. Created between 1935 and 1942 under the Index of American Design, this artwork records the rich religious and folk art heritage of the American Southwest. The piece captures the stylized flames and symbolic imagery characteristic of historical Spanish colonial craftsmanship.

Peace Dove
Leo Gestel · ca. 1934–1936
Rijksmuseum

Created between 1934 and 1936, Peace Dove is a compelling graphic drawing by the Dutch modernist artist Leo Gestel. Originally designed as a book illustration, the work captures the universal symbol of peace through bold, fluid strokes. Gestel's approach synthesizes organic form with expressive draftsmanship, translating the gentle bird into a dynamic and timeless graphic emblem.

post_title: The moon of The Starry Night (1889)
post_content:
[section label="Contact" bg="519" bg_overlay="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.488)" bg_pos="94% 27%" padding="90px" padding__sm="60px"] [row h_align="center" padding="0px 0px 0px 0px"] [col span="9" span__sm="12" span__md="8" padding="70px 70px 30px 70px" padding__sm="50px 30px 10px 30px" margin="110px 0px 0px 0px" align="center" bg_color="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.471)" bg_radius="10" text_depth="1" border="0px 0px 0px 0px" border_margin="0px 0px 0px 0px"] [ux_text line_height="1.65" text_color="rgb(0, 0, 0)"]

Origin & Meaning

Starry Night (Moon Detail)
Vincent van Gogh · 1889
Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), New York

Originally painted in 1889, The Starry Night by Vincent van Gogh is one of the most recognized works of Post-Impressionism. This design draws inspiration from the swirling moon motif in the night sky. The circular movement and rhythmic brush energy transform the sky into motion, turning light into a living, emotional presence rather than a static celestial body.

[/ux_text] [gap height="10px"] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

Origin & Meaning

Angels Care
Paul Klee · 1931
Zentrum Paul Klee, Bern

Created in 1931, Angels Care is a work by Paul Klee exploring the poetic language of line and symbol. The composition features simplified angelic forms rendered with delicate contour and subtle color. Rather than literal religious depiction, Klee transforms the figure into a quiet, intimate presence, reflecting early twentieth-century modernism’s search for spiritual expression through abstraction.

[/ux_text] [gap height="10px"] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

Peace

The composition reflects the graphic clarity and poster aesthetics of the late twentieth century. Rather than detailed realism, the design relies on bold silhouette and direct symbolism, creating a calm yet striking visual statement centered on hope and unity.

post_title: We Take the Stars from Heaven (1776)
post_content:
[section label="Contact" bg="501" bg_overlay="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.488)" bg_pos="37% 49%" padding="90px" padding__sm="60px"] [gap] [row h_align="center" padding="0px 0px 0px 0px"] [col span="9" span__sm="12" span__md="8" padding="70px 70px 30px 70px" padding__sm="50px 30px 10px 30px" margin="110px 0px 0px 0px" align="center" bg_color="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.471)" bg_radius="10" text_depth="1" border="0px 0px 0px 0px" border_margin="0px 0px 0px 0px"] [ux_text line_height="1.65" text_color="rgb(0, 0, 0)"]

Origin & Meaning

We Take the Stars from Heaven
Broadside Print · 1776
Library of Congress

Created in 1776, this printed broadside combines poetic language with symbolic imagery of stars and color. The phrase “We take the stars from heaven…” evokes aspiration, imagination, and the idea of reaching upward. Rather than functioning as a decorative artwork alone, the piece carries a sense of optimism and visual clarity, transforming simple elements into a statement about vision and possibility.

[/ux_text] [gap height="10px"] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

Origin & Meaning

Woman and Flower
Mikuláš Galanda · 1937
Slovenská národná galéria (SNG)

Created in 1937, Woman and Flower is a modernist painting by Slovak artist Mikuláš Galanda. The composition merges the female figure with simplified floral forms, reflecting the expressive and symbolic tendencies of interwar modernism. Rather than presenting a literal portrait, the image condenses emotion and gesture into stylized contours and restrained color relationships.

[/ux_text] [gap height="10px"] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

Free Curve to the Point
Wassily Kandinsky · 1925
Rijksmuseum

Created in 1925, Free Curve to the Point is a work by Wassily Kandinsky, a pioneer of abstract art. The composition explores the relationship between curved lines, geometric forms, and visual rhythm. Rather than depicting objects, Kandinsky transforms line and color into expressive structure, reflecting the early twentieth-century search for spiritual and formal harmony through abstraction.

[/ux_text] [gap height="10px"] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

Blad met banenpatroon van harten
Unknown · 1750–1900
Rijksmuseum

This decorative sheet, created between 1750 and 1900, presents a repeating striped pattern of stylized hearts. The design reflects the ornamental print traditions of early modern Europe, where rhythm and symmetry were central to surface decoration. Rather than telling a story, the image focuses on repetition and visual balance, turning a simple heart motif into a structured, almost textile-like composition.

Volcano

Slovenská národná galéria (SNG)

Created in 1930, Volcano is a pen-and-ink work by Slovak modernist Mikuláš Galanda. The composition merges volcanic form with simplified symbolic elements, reflecting early twentieth-century modernist experimentation. Rather than depicting a literal landscape, the image compresses nature into sharp contours and compact structure, emphasizing tension and controlled energy.

Origin & Meaning

Phases of Mercury Illustration
Agnes Giberne · 1898
Source: The Story of the Sun, Moon, and Stars

This illustration appears in the 1898 astronomy book The Story of the Sun, Moon, and Stars by Agnes Giberne and depicts the phases of Mercury before inferior conjunction — the moment when the planet lies between Earth and the Sun. In the late 19th century, visual diagrams like this were key to translating complex celestial motion into accessible imagery. The sequence reads like a visual rhythm, turning planetary motion into a simple graphic progression that bridges science and artistic intuition.

[/ux_text] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

[section label="Media Bottom" bg="463" bg_color="rgb(208, 208, 208)" bg_overlay="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.85)" bg_pos="60% 30%" padding="0px"] [row style="collapse" h_align="center"] [col span="6" span__sm="12" padding="50px 0px 50px 0px" align="center"] [ux_text text_color="rgb(0,0,0)"]

Origin & Meaning

Broken Heart Illustration
Alias: Heartbreak Emblem · Emotional Fracture Symbol
Author: Unknown · 19th century
Collection: Library of Congress

This illustration centers on the motif of a fractured heart to convey emotional rupture, loss, and distress. In modern visual culture, the broken heart has become a widely recognized symbol of heartbreak—often associated with separation, betrayal, or the end of an intimate bond.

The heart shape has long functioned as a sign of affection and love across symbolic traditions. Its fragmentation transforms that meaning into a universal emblem of absence and emotional fracture, shifting the language of love into the language of separation.

[/ux_text] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

[section label="Contact" bg="455" bg_overlay="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.488)" bg_pos="37% 49%" padding="90px" padding__sm="60px"] [gap] [row h_align="center" padding="0px 0px 0px 0px"] [col span="9" span__sm="12" span__md="8" padding="70px 70px 30px 70px" padding__sm="50px 30px 10px 30px" margin="110px 0px 0px 0px" align="center" bg_color="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.471)" bg_radius="10" text_depth="1" border="0px 0px 0px 0px" border_margin="0px 0px 0px 0px"] [ux_text line_height="1.65" text_color="rgb(0, 0, 0)"]

Origin & Meaning

Meisje knuffelt een kat
Theo van Hoytema · c. 1890–1910
Collection: Rijksmuseum

This late nineteenth- to early twentieth-century work by Dutch illustrator Theo van Hoytema depicts a girl gently holding a cat in a compact, intimate composition. Known for his animal imagery and decorative linear style, Van Hoytema emphasized clear contours and flattened space. The figures form a stable structure, focusing on balance and restraint rather than narrative drama. The image reflects a broader tradition of domestic themes in Dutch illustration, presenting everyday intimacy with clarity and compositional order.

[/ux_text] [gap height="10px"] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

post_content:
[section label="Media Bottom" bg="411" bg_color="rgb(208, 208, 208)" bg_overlay="rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.85)" padding="0px"] [row style="collapse" h_align="center"] [col span="6" span__sm="12" padding="50px 0px 50px 0px" align="center"] [ux_text text_color="rgb(0,0,0)"]

Origin & Meaning

Crying Woman
Author: Mikuláš Galanda · 1938
Collection: Slovak National Gallery

This 1938 work by Slovak modernist Mikuláš Galanda, Crying Woman (Plačúca žena), is intensely abstract: the female figure twists into flowing waves, hair raging like a storm, black lines carving a torn face—hollow eyes, mouth stretched in silent scream. Her hands reach from above, cradling massive crimson cloths or handkerchiefs, tears dripping as blood-red spots, as if bleeding from the soul. Red dominates, symbolizing raw trauma and passion; grief is ritualized, held up so it won’t spill uselessly into the void. Rough lines and stark contrasts evoke a pre-apocalyptic hush, turning personal anguish into an eternal, undulating lament.

[/ux_text] [/col] [/row] [/section] [block id="305"]

Painter.ink

Last Updated: March 2026
Contact: hi@painter.ink

1. Order Processing

Orders are typically processed within 1–3 business days after payment confirmation.

Processing times may vary during peak periods or due to high demand.

2. Delivery Time

Estimated delivery times vary by destination and carrier.

Most international orders arrive within 7–20 business days, depending on the country and customs clearance process.

Please note that delivery times are estimates and are not guaranteed.

Delays caused by customs inspections, local carriers, weather conditions, or other unforeseen circumstances are beyond our control.

3. Shipping Fees

We use a fixed shipping rate for all international orders to keep pricing simple and transparent.

The exact shipping fee is displayed at checkout before payment.

Shipping fees are non-refundable once an order has been dispatched.

4. Customs, Duties & Taxes

International shipments may be subject to import duties, taxes, or customs fees determined by the destination country.

These charges are the responsibility of the customer.

Painter.ink does not collect or control these fees.

5. Tracking Information

Tracking details will be provided once the order has been shipped.

Please allow 24–72 hours for tracking information to update.

6. Address Accuracy

Customers are responsible for providing accurate shipping information.

Orders returned due to incorrect or incomplete addresses may require additional shipping fees for reshipment.

7. Delays Beyond Our Control

Painter.ink is not responsible for delays caused by customs, carriers, natural events, or other circumstances beyond our control.

Painter.ink

Last Updated: March 2026
Contact: hi@painter.ink

1. General

By accessing or purchasing from Painter.ink, you agree to these Terms & Conditions.

We reserve the right to update or modify these terms at any time without prior notice.

2. Product Information

We strive to present product images and descriptions as accurately as possible.
However, slight variations in color, size, or appearance may occur due to screen differences or production factors.

3. Pricing

All prices are listed in USD unless otherwise stated.

We reserve the right to adjust pricing without notice.

4. Orders & Payments

All payments are securely processed through trusted third-party providers such as Stripe and PayPal.

We reserve the right to refuse or cancel orders suspected of fraud or abuse.

5. Shipping & Delivery

Shipping estimates are provided in our Shipping Policy.
Delivery times are not guaranteed.

6. Returns & Refunds

Return and refund terms are outlined in our Refund Policy.

7. Intellectual Property

All website content, including text, graphics, and design elements, is the property of Painter.ink unless otherwise stated.

Public-domain artwork remains legally public domain, but our selection, restoration, and presentation constitute original creative work.

8. Limitation of Liability

Painter.ink shall not be liable for indirect, incidental, or consequential damages arising from the use of our products or website.

9. Governing Law

These Terms & Conditions shall be governed by applicable international commercial practices.

Painter.ink explores public-domain artworks released by leading museums and cultural institutions around the world, as well as works clearly licensed under CC0.

From extensive historical archives, we select pieces with strong aesthetic and decorative value. Our team restores and reinterprets them through line refinement, detail reconstruction, color balancing, and compositional adjustments — allowing classic imagery to adapt naturally to modern life.

Each design begins with respect for the original artwork and ends with thoughtful reinterpretation for contemporary use.

All imagery is presented purely for artistic purposes. It does not represent any religious, political, cultural, or ethnic stance. Historical elements reflect their original artistic context.

Community Contribution

Art appreciation varies from person to person.

If you discover a public-domain artwork that aligns with our vision, you are welcome to share it with us at:

📩 hi@painter.ink

We carefully review every submission.

Supporting Cultural Institutions

We value the museums and institutions that make public art resources accessible to the world.

At regular intervals, we allocate part of our revenue to support cultural institutions connected to the artworks that inspire our designs. We also purchase selected official merchandise from these institutions and share them with our customers.

Our goal is simple: to keep art circulating in meaningful ways.

Product Safety

All physical products are made primarily from plant-based materials, are non-toxic, and meet applicable safety standards.

Painter.ink

Last Updated: March 2026
Contact: hi@painter.ink

1. Order Issues

If your item arrives damaged, defective, or incorrect, please contact us within 7 days of delivery.

We will review your case and provide a reasonable solution.

2. Replacement or Refund

Depending on the situation, we may offer:

  • A replacement
  • A partial refund
  • Or a full refund

The final decision is based on order details and available evidence.

3. Required Information

To process your request, please provide:

  • Order number
  • Clear photos of the issue

4. Shipping & Delivery

Delivery times are estimates and may vary due to customs clearance or carrier delays.

Shipping fees are non-refundable once the order has been dispatched.

5. Change of Mind

We do not accept returns or refunds due to:

  • Change of mind
  • Personal preference
  • Incorrect address provided at checkout

6. Lost Packages

If a package is confirmed lost by the carrier, we will provide an appropriate solution.

7. Contact

For assistance, email: hi@painter.ink

Painter.ink

Last updated: [2026.2.28]

1. Overview

At Painter.ink, we respect your privacy and are committed to protecting your personal information. This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, and safeguard your data when you visit our website or make a purchase.

By using our website, you agree to the practices described in this policy.

2. Information We Collect

When you visit or make a purchase, we may collect:

  • Name
  • Email address
  • Shipping address
  • Billing address
  • Phone number
  • Order details
  • IP address and browser information

We do not store or process your full credit card details. Payments are securely processed by third-party providers.

3. How We Use Your Information

We use your information to:

  • Process and fulfill orders
  • Arrange shipping and delivery
  • Communicate about your order
  • Provide customer support
  • Improve our website and services
  • Prevent fraud and abuse

We do not sell or rent your personal information.

4. Payment Processing

All payments are securely processed through:

  • Stripe
  • PayPal

These providers handle your payment information according to their own privacy policies. Painter.ink does not store credit card data.

5. Shipping & Third-Party Services

To fulfill orders, we may share necessary information with:

  • Shipping carriers
  • Logistics providers
  • Fraud prevention services

Information shared is limited to what is required to complete your order.

6. Cookies & Tracking

Our website uses cookies to:

  • Enable shopping cart functionality
  • Remember user preferences
  • Improve performance and user experience

You may disable cookies in your browser settings, but some features may not function properly.

7. Data Retention

We retain order information for:

  • Accounting purposes
  • Legal compliance
  • Customer support

You may request deletion of your personal data, subject to legal requirements.

8. Data Security

We implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to protect your information. However, no online transmission can be guaranteed 100% secure.

9. Your Rights

Depending on your location, you may have rights to:

  • Access your personal data
  • Request correction
  • Request deletion
  • Object to processing

To exercise your rights, contact us.

10. Contact Us

If you have questions about this Privacy Policy, please contact:

📩 hi@painter.ink
